source: Yahoo Mail 'Substack' folder via IMAP; summarized server-side

bullets: Tensions between the US and Iran are escalating, with experts predicting a major US escalation against Iran soon, amidst Trump's recent Iran war rhetoric and US strikes on alleged 'narcoterrorist' drug boats

Trump's administration is facing criticism for its handling of various issues, including the ICE killing of a Colombian immigrant in Maine and the president's admission of a corrupt plan to influence CNN's coverage
The US is experiencing a surge in cyclospora cases, with over 3,000 reported, and a potentially severe West Nile virus season, prompting concerns about the country's public health infrastructure

Corporate tax rates have declined significantly, benefiting wealthy shareholders, while Democrats are being criticized for their inability to effectively communicate their policies and values to voters
Trump's record of defending accused sex predators has been highlighted, amidst ongoing controversy over his own allegations and the GOP's hypocrisy on the issue

The Supreme Court's recent ruling on independent agencies has raised concerns about the erosion of democratic institutions and the need for reforms to protect these agencies from partisan interference

items.subject: ICE’s New Permanent Family Separation: Killing Latino Fathers
bullets: ICE agents shot 26‑year‑old Johan Sebastián Durán Guerrero in Biddeford, Maine, claiming public‑safety concerns despite his legal work status and presence of his 3‑year‑old daughter at the scene.

The shooting mirrors the recent killing of Lorenzo Salgado Araujo; both victims were fathers, agents lacked body‑cameras despite a $20 million congressional allocation, and a new recruit fired the shots.
Latino leaders and activists, including Rep. Joaquin Castro and influencer Carlos Espina, condemn the killings as state‑sanctioned violence targeting community providers and demand accountability.

items.subject: "I Saw Terrorization of the Palestinians": U.S. Congressman Detained by Settlers
bullets: California Congressman Ro Khanna was detained by Israeli settlers and soldiers during a Palestinian‑led West Bank visit, describing the experience as “terrorization of the Palestinians.”

Khanna says the detention sparked bipartisan attacks and criticism, accusing U.S. ambassador Mike Huckabee of failing to protect American citizens and fearing his trip will set a precedent for others.
He explains his shift from pro‑Israel views, refuses to endorse a full arms embargo on Israel, and notes Elon Musk threatened to sue him over related legislation.

items.subject: The Emperor’s Tollbooth
bullets: Trump announced a 20% toll on all cargo through the Strait of Hormuz, then retracted it hours later, proposing vague “investments” from Gulf states instead, a move the author calls an act of war and evidence of presidential madness
The author argues the plan was never realistic because no major powers—China, Europe, Indo‑Pacific allies—reacted, indicating the proposal was detached from maritime law and international feasibility

items.subject: Pete Buttigieg: Time for Radical Change
bullets: Pete Buttigieg wants radical institutional changes
He proposes expanding SCOTUS and the House
Buttigieg is stumping for candidates in Fox News areas

items.subject: ‘Major U.S. Escalation’ Against Iran Soon, Experts Tell Zeteo
bullets: Trita Parsi expects a major US escalation against Iran soon
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi mocked Trump's 20% toll demand
Rami Khouri calls the Lebanon-Israel peace deal a neo-colonial affirmation

items.newsletter: Bill Kristol & Andrew Egger - The Bulwark
items.subject: Trump Keeps Bombing, Nothing Keeps Changing.
bullets: Trump’s “participation trophy” at the National Mall is a 10‑foot monument to his Iran war, installed by an anonymous protest‑art group near the MLK Memorial.

Mark Hertling argues that U.S. military superiority over Iran is offset by Iran’s ability to disrupt shipping, energy markets, and U.S. bases, creating a mission‑creep scenario where punishment does not equal coercion.
William Kristol uses Burke’s idea of a “lovely” country to critique ICE killings and the failure of Todd Blanche to seek justice for Epstein survivors, calling for congressional action.

items.newsletter: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
items.subject: Trump's Stunning Admission About CNN
bullets: Trump admitted on live TV that the FCC’s approval of Paramount Skydance’s acquisition of CNN was part of a corrupt plan to make the network more MAGA‑friendly, citing pro‑Trump billionaire David Ellison’s promises to change CNN’s coverage and lineup, including hiring Bari Weiss to overhaul the network’s news programs.

The author argues that Democrats are outgunned in today’s information war because pro‑Trump billionaires own major media outlets and social platforms, and urges Democrats to invest in independent, pro‑democracy media infrastructure rather than traditional TV to compete effectively.

items.subject: ‘You Took Her Dad’: ICE Kills Maine Man in Front of Daughter in Bluey Pajamas
bullets: ICE agents in Biddeford, Maine, shot and killed 26‑year‑old Colombian immigrant Joan Sebastian Guerrero, who had work authorization and a Social Security number, after a chaotic confrontation that the agency later described as a “fleeing” attempt and a “weaponized” vehicle claim.

Maine Senator Angus King revealed that DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin admitted Guerrero was not the target of a warrant, and the department subsequently altered its narrative to justify the shooting as a response to a perceived threat to public safety.

The incident underscores ongoing controversies over ICE enforcement tactics under the Trump administration, amid broader criticism of the agency’s use of force and the administration’s handling of immigration policy.

items.subject: Suddenly, Hormuz is Less Crucial Than It Was
bullets: Trump’s brinkmanship over the Strait of Hormuz has limited impact on oil markets because global refining capacity, not crude supply, is the bottleneck, driven by a 10% loss from Russian refinery attacks linked to the Ukraine war.
Even though crude prices fell near pre‑war levels, consumer‑facing gasoline and diesel prices remain about $75 per barrel due to refinery shortages, keeping demand low and inflation sticky.
The brief Hormuz crisis illustrates how U.S. policy driven by Trump’s vanity adds geopolitical risk without altering the underlying supply‑demand dynamics shaped by the Russia‑Ukraine conflict.

items.subject: Artificial Bloom
bullets: Bloom Energy’s solid‑oxide fuel cells promise cheap, clean power from US natural gas, but their growth hinges on scarce scandium, a rare‑earth largely supplied by China.
Short‑seller Hunterbrook traced four China‑linked scandium supply routes, arguing Bloom’s plan for 25 GW of cells would require multiples of global scandium output, making its expansion unrealistic.
Bloom rebutted in an SEC 8‑K, claiming sufficient non‑Chinese scandium supply and visibility to meet current and future demand, though skeptics note the claim’s credibility remains unproven.

items.subject: LIVE IN LONDON! Get Tickets for Zeteo UK’s Launch Event with Mehdi and Zack Polanski
bullets: Zeteo UK, the fastest‑growing new Substack in its first 30 days, will host a live launch on 14 September at the Barbican Centre featuring an interview with Green Party leader Zack Polanski and a Q&A on Labour’s prospects, Green influence, Reform UK, Middle‑East politics and Donald Trump
Subscribers receive early ticket access before the public release on 20 June, with paid subscribers and Founding Members getting a 20% discount and a bundle subscription option for future UK events

items.subject: Cyclospora keeps climbing, a bad West Nile season, truck spraying, and good news
bullets: Cyclospora cases are surging, with Michigan reporting 2,640 cases—31 times the usual level—and investigators suspect lettuce or packaged salads, so avoid bagged greens until more data emerge

The CDC has flagged an unusually early West Nile virus spike—48 cases across 23 states, over five times the historical average for this time—prompting mosquito‑control trucks to spray, which studies show poses minimal health risk
Over 341,000 public comments were submitted on the OMB’s proposed rule that could undermine U.S. science, marking an unprecedented response before the comment period closed

items.subject: 'No More Wars?' Mehdi Challenges Top Trump Ally Over the 'Erratic' President's Betrayal on Iran
bullets: Mehdi accuses Trump of breaking his 2024 campaign promise of “no more wars,” citing Trump’s own words and arguing the president’s recent Iran rhetoric is a dangerous, illegal war revival.

He confronts Piers Morgan on Trump’s alleged “exit ramp” strategy, claiming escalating conflict is unwise, and criticizes mainstream media for hagiography of deceased figures while ignoring Senator Lindsey Graham’s alleged bloodshed.

items.subject: The Lindsey Graham Conspiracy Theories Are Already Running Wild
bullets: Right‑wing activists Laura Loomer, Matt Van Swol, and columnist Marc Thiessen spun Senator Lindsey Graham’s aortic‑dissection death into conspiracies about Russian or Iranian assassinations, despite his long‑standing blood‑pressure issues.

Billionaire Bill Ackman and former CBS reporter Lara Logan joined the speculation, with Ackman deleting a tweet questioning the death’s plausibility and Logan hunting numerological clues in Trump’s posts.

The episode illustrates how, in 2026’s MAGA‑driven online sphere, any unexpected event—like a 71‑year‑old senator’s natural death—is immediately reframed as evidence of hidden plots, echoing academic Michael Barkun’s claim that conspiracists reject coincidence.

items.subject: When Will Democrats Learn the Importance of a Good Fight, Not Just a Good Policy?
bullets: Democrats have repeatedly failed to win elections by focusing on good policy and compromise, assuming voters reward virtue; Obama’s own admissions show a lack of effective messaging and storytelling.

The Biden administration’s risk‑averse, incremental approach—seen in weak labor enforcement, cautious green‑energy funding, and delayed Jan. 6 investigations—illustrates a broader Democratic fear of taking bold, confrontational actions.

Citing a study of 48 former officials, the author argues that Democrats’ reluctance to “pick the fights worth having” stems from a misplaced belief that the political center will reward centrism, a mindset that has cost them political power.

items.subject: Lindsey Graham: The Good, the Bad, and the Banality of Evil
bullets: Democratic figures, including Amy Klobuchar, praised Lindsey Graham as a devoted, energetic senator who embraced tough issues and remained loyal to friends and causes.
The author acknowledges the heartfelt tributes but warns that Graham’s death should also prompt harsher, more rigorous political analysis.

items.subject: 💥6pm ET TODAY! What’s Next for Iran, Palestine, and Lebanon Q&A
bullets: A live Town Hall Q&A on July 13 at 6 pm ET will examine the recent U.S. strikes on Iran, Iran’s retaliation across Gulf allies, and the collapse of the cease‑fire.

Panelists—including Trita Parsi, Rami Khouri, and Rula Jebreal—will discuss escalating conflicts in Iran, Lebanon’s fragile peace with Israel, and the worsening humanitarian crisis and settler violence in Gaza and the West Bank.
House member Ro Khanna reported being detained by Israeli settlers armed with U.S.-made M4 rifles during a recent West Bank visit.

items.newsletter: Bill Kristol & Andrew Egger - The Bulwark
items.subject: Jeffrey Epstein Takes Center Stage
bullets: Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche is accused of orchestrating a cover‑up of the Jeffrey Epstein case, meeting Ghislaine Maxwell, overseeing botched redactions, and now faces a Senate Judiciary hearing for AG confirmation.

A poll shows 75% of Americans, including 66% of Republicans, believe the government is still hiding Epstein information, and Democrats are urged to spotlight Blanche’s role to sway voters ahead of the hearing.

items.subject: Trump Makes Lindsey Graham’s Death All About... Himself
bullets: Trump framed Senator Lindsey Graham’s death as a personal victory, recalling Graham’s 2016 primary challenge and praising his later support for Trump’s agenda, especially the SAVE Act.

The newsletter notes Graham’s 2024 death from a heart attack and contrasts his early criticism of Trump—calling him a “nutjob” and “demagogue”—with his later alignment on issues like the 2018 Kavanaugh defense.
It also highlights that the SAVE Act, Trump’s voter‑suppression proposal, is set to dominate congressional discussion when Congress reconvenes on Monday.

items.subject: Oligarchy in Action: The Case of Corporate Tax Cuts
bullets: Corporate tax rates fell from about 35% in the 1960s to roughly 12% today, a decline that primarily benefits wealthy shareholders and coincides with a sharp rise in top‑0.01% wealth concentration.

Gallup polls show a persistent majority of Americans think corporations pay too little in taxes, yet corporate tax cuts have continued, driven by campaign finance influence, corruption, and a media ecosystem that normalizes billionaire‑friendly policies.

The 2017 Trump corporate tax cut produced no significant increase in U.S. investment, reinforcing the view that such cuts do not trickle down and instead serve as indirect tax cuts for the rich, underscoring the need for Democratic policies to raise taxes on the wealthy.

items.subject: One Weird Trick for Torpedoing Todd Blanche’s Hearing
bullets: Democratic senators have a litany of accusations to use against Attorney General‑designate Todd Blanche, including conflicts of interest, DOJ purges, a slush fund, Jan. 6 pardons, attempted self‑pardons, politically‑charged prosecutions, a “war” on judges, and obstruction of oversight.
The author argues that while all these issues merit scrutiny, focusing on a single, especially damaging point could be the most effective strategy to undermine Blanche’s confirmation.
Specific evidence links Blanche to controversial actions such as the Jan. 6 pardons and attempts to self‑pardon, suggesting a pattern of politicized legal maneuvering.

items.subject: Trump’s Illegal Boat Bombings Are Just ‘Murder for Murder’s Sake,’ U.S. Official Says
bullets: U.S. officials say Trump’s administration continues a lethal bombing campaign against alleged “narcoterrorist” drug boats, despite lacking evidence of targets and facing accusations of illegality.
Intelligence and government data cited by the newsletter indicate the strikes have not reduced drug trafficking or saved lives, and the White House is aware of their ineffectiveness.
President Trump has reportedly discussed expanding the operations, claiming they make the U.S. safer and prevent tens of thousands of deaths, though no verification of casualties exists.

items.subject: We Found Decades of Video of Trump Defending Accused Sex Predators
bullets: Sarah Longwell and Tim Miller detail how Donald Trump repeatedly defended men accused of sexual assault—from Roy Moore and Roger Ailes to Mike Tyson and Ghislaine Maxwell—showing a pattern of GOP hypocrisy.

items.subject: Lindsey Graham, Proud War Hawk, Has Died. Here He Is in His Own Words
bullets: Lindsey Graham, a Republican senator and prominent war hawk, died suddenly at 71 from cardiac arrest, days before his expected re-election bid.
Graham's career was marked by shifting stances, from harshly criticizing Trump in 2015-2021 to calling him 'the greatest president' by 2026.
He advocated extreme policies, including 'leveling Gaza,' assassinating Putin, and defending Trump's 2020 election lies, while praising genocidal Israeli ministers posthumously.

items.subject: Republicans Slam Platner, but Stay Silent About Trump’s Assault Allegations
bullets: Republicans publicly condemned Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner after a sexual assault allegation, yet remain silent on similar accusations against former President Trump.
Francesca Fiorentini highlights the double standard, noting that GOP figures like Kevin McCarthy and Sen. John Kennedy criticize attacks on women while defending a known alleged predator.
In a three‑minute video, the most empathetic Republican toward Platner is identified, underscoring the party’s inconsistent stance on sexual misconduct.

items.subject: The Supreme Court Broke Independent Agencies. Here’s a Way to Slow the Damage.
bullets: Guest author Todd Phillips: the Court's Trump v. Slaughter ruling overturned 91-year-old Humphrey's Executor, letting presidents fire FTC/NLRB/CPSC/EEOC commissioners over policy disagreements.
His fix: a crypto market-structure bill already before Congress could carry provisions keeping independent commissions from becoming one-party instruments.

items.subject: AI in an Age of Oligarchy
bullets: Krugman: AI is arriving into wealth concentration worse than the Gilded Age — unlike the 1950s-60s, when progressive taxes and antitrust could have contained the damage.
The oligarchic context — weak worker protections, captured policy — will magnify AI's social and economic downsides rather than spreading its gains.

items.subject: Explosive foodborne outbreak
bullets: Katelyn Jetelina on the largest cyclosporiasis outbreak in U.S. history: 3,000+ cases (a normal YEAR's total) and climbing, spread via a rinse-resistant parasite on produce.
State counts are running ahead of the CDC's website — she blames a gutted federal public-health apparatus with no transparency or centralized communication for the confusing guidance.

items.subject: This Week in Palestine: An Emotional Reunion After Israel Releases Palestinian Captive Believed to Be Dead
bullets: Weekly roundup: Israel escalated Gaza attacks, killing at least two dozen including children and two aid workers; West Bank settler violence is up 560% since 2019 with 6.6% of cases indicted.
One bright spot: an emotional reunion after Israel released a Palestinian captive his family believed was dead.

items.subject: Socialist Arizona Candidate Says Israel Is Interfering in His Election
bullets: Democratic socialist Kai Newkirk, challenging Rep. Greg Stanton in Arizona, tells Zeteo Israel is interfering in his primary — extending the Mamdani-style insurgent wave to a new state.
He calls Stanton's record enabling the Gaza war 'morally disqualifying.'

items.subject: Exclusive: The Next Frontier of the Deportation Wars: College Campuses
bullets: Exclusive: activists are opening a new front against Flock license-plate cameras on college campuses, whose data feeds ICE target-hunting — an ambient dragnet catching immigrants on daily commutes.
Follow-up: the GoFundMe for Lorenzo Salgado Araujo's family jumped from $58,000 to $425,000 after last week's newsletter on his killing by ICE.

items.subject: Tommy Vietor: Democrats’ Way Forward on Israel
bullets: Tommy Vietor joins the Focus Group: Democratic voters' opinion of Israel has nosedived over three years, and Israel politics is now shaping 2026 primaries and the next presidential field.

items.subject: “I had become haunted by the simplest question: What did I care about?”
bullets: Substack's Weekender: an essay on quitting and staying in New York ('What did I care about?'), writers debating AI's merits, and a dispatch from NBA summer league.

items.newsletter: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
items.subject: How Democrats Can Still Beat Susan Collins
bullets: Pfeiffer's mailbag: Platner's exit actually leaves Maine winnable — Collins' campaign built its whole strategy around him, and a vetted progressive replacement scrambles it.
He owns what he missed on Platner and points to his Pod Save America conversation with Alex Wagner for the postmortem.

items.subject: Policy in Search of a Problem
bullets: Overtime digest: Mark Hertling argues withdrawing U.S. troops from Europe is 'a policy in search of a problem' as DOD reviews force posture during the Ankara NATO summit.
Also: evangelicals confronting Christian nationalism in their own churches, and Eric Edelman on the one word Trump won't say in Turkey.

items.subject: Dennis Kelleher of Better Markets
bullets: Krugman interviews Better Markets' Dennis Kelleher on the quiet demolition of financial regulation — 'termites' eating stability while attention is elsewhere.
Kelleher's case: financial-crisis risk lands on everyone, not just the investor class that owns most assets.

items.subject: This Week in Democracy – Week 77: ICE Kills Again, White Supremacists Descend on DC, and Trump Wants Birthright Ci…
bullets: Week 77 roundup: Trump fired the remaining Democratic election commissioners (post-Slaughter), ordered more Iran strikes mid-NATO-summit, and pardoned 11 including a $4M GOP donor tied to Abramoff.
Also this week: Patriot Front white supremacists marched through DC on July 4, and the administration is pushing to have the birthright-citizenship case reheard.

items.subject: Trump Did What? (Evergreen Tweet.)
bullets: Sarah and JVL on Trump's NATO disgrace, whether McConnell is really working the phones on policy, and the Democratic factional fight — is strategist Morris Katz just the left's Steve Bannon?

items.subject: R.I.P. Attention Spans
bullets: Bulwark Goes to Hollywood interviews critic David Thomson on his new book 'A Sudden Flicker of Light' — film's amoral artistry has 'begun to diminish our nature.'
Thomson calls Citizen Kane a 'shallow masterpiece' and traces the attention-span rot from packed 1930 movie palaces to TV to the phone in your pocket.

items.subject: Trump Could Move U.S. Bases to Israel. Here’s Why They’d Become Netanyahu’s Newest Human Shields
bullets: Former defense intel analyst Harrison Mann: the Pentagon, after evacuating damaged HQs in Kuwait, Bahrain and Qatar, is exploring big permanent U.S. bases inside Israel.
He argues they'd become Netanyahu's 'human shields' — empowering Israeli aggression and permanently entangling the U.S. — while Congress advances a bill merging U.S.-Israeli military R&D.

items.subject: The Red Menace Is Here!
bullets: The White House is promoting 'Freedom Fuel,' a mystery chain selling below-market gas whose trademark was filed the same day Trump first posted about it.
JVL: the 'red menace' is Republican — a state-adjacent enterprise with zero transparency; no one can say who owns it or how it sells gas at a loss.

items.newsletter: Bill Kristol & Andrew Egger - The Bulwark
items.subject: A Season of Death and Fear
bullets: Jim Swift reports from Springfield, Ohio: Haitian TPS work authorization expired today after Mullin v. Doe — employers must now fire longtime workers or violate federal law.
Navigator poll: nearly half of Americans have watched a 2026 World Cup game, with identical enthusiasm among Democrats and Republicans.

items.subject: Does Trump Compare To Hitler? Mehdi Asks This Fascism Expert
bullets: Mehdi interviews Omer Aziz ('Shadows of the Republic'): 'the much more dangerous part of fascism is the fascists in suits' — constitutional machinery turned against citizens.
They cover whether Trump compares to Hitler, Musk's role in the global fascist project, and Aziz's three-point antifascist playbook.

items.newsletter: Douglas Macgregor from Warrior Diplomacy Substack
items.subject: A ‘Golden Spike’ to Transform America from Coast to Coast
bullets: Macgregor republishes his 2018 op-ed pitching a 'golden spike' energy-maritime-infrastructure program as the strategic alternative to Obama-era piecemeal spending (paywalled past the intro).

items.subject: 🆘 Trump’s Violent Crackdowns on U.S. Cities Are Still Happening. Don't Look Away
bullets: Swin: Trump's violent crackdowns on immigrants and blue cities are still escalating after the Minneapolis horror forced a PR 'reset' — the strategy is to wear the public out.
Graham Platner's campaign confirms he'll formally withdraw from the Maine Senate race on Monday.

items.subject: Trump's Own Judge Torched DOJ's Insane Election Subpoena (w/ Andrew Weissmann)
bullets: Sarah Longwell and Andrew Weissmann on the Trump-appointed judge who torched DOJ's subpoena demanding names of every 2020 Fulton County election worker.
Also: DOJ's stunning concession in a reporter's Epstein-files lawsuit, what senators must ask Todd Blanche, and a surprising SCOTUS ruling on phone location data.

items.subject: An Encouraging Encounter With Real Americans
bullets: Krugman reports for jury selection in Mercer County, NJ, and finds the mundane civic machinery — 78 people patiently waiting to state conflicts — quietly encouraging about Americans.

items.subject: Sources and Methods
bullets: Doomberg: energy-war analysis is now propaganda discernment — the FT's 'apocalyptic' footage of Ukraine's June 18 strike on Moscow's biggest refinery says little about real damage to Russian output.

items.subject: The Price of Staying on Trump’s Good Side
bullets: Trump claimed Walmart cut ground-beef prices ~15% 'at his administration's request' — it was a routine July Rollback promotion already underway.
Rampell: companies are learning that letting Trump take credit is the price of staying on his good side — free marketing for them, corroded markets for everyone else.

items.newsletter: Zeteo | Dark Side of the World Cup
items.subject: The Dark Side of the World Cup: Balogun and Egypt VAR Decisions Fuel Allegations of Corruption
bullets: Final 'Dark Side of the World Cup' episode ties Trump pressuring Infantino to reverse Balogun's red card, and VAR calls against Egypt and Iran, to FIFA's long corruption history.
Jules Boykoff: the World Cup is where 'a privileged sliver of the global 1%... cut deals with each other' in the luxury boxes.

items.subject: ‘A First-of-Its-Kind’ Poll About Medicare for All
bullets: Zeteo partnered with centrist Searchlight Institute on a first-of-its-kind Medicare for All poll: single-payer draws majority or plurality support depending on wording.
Strong support for government covering healthcare costs sits alongside deep skepticism that government can run a national program.

items.subject: The Drinking Culture on Capitol Hill is Worse Than You Might Think
bullets: Guest author Jake Lahut maps Capitol Hill's drinking ecosystem: female staffers keep whisper networks of which bars — and which booze-drenched lawmakers — to avoid.
The culture underpins abuse scandals like Platner's and Swalwell's; Twain complained about DC booze in 1889 and it's only gotten worse.

items.subject: Progressives Are Mad at the Wrong People
bullets: JVL: progressives raging at the Democratic establishment (or Israel, or oligarchs) over Platner's implosion are misreading it — an imperfect establishment is still the only vehicle against Trumpism.
Conflating the fight for Medicare for All with the fight against authoritarianism is 'a grievous mistake.'

items.subject: ‘Death’ and ‘Mass Murder’: Abby Martin on U.S. Policy Toward Cuba
bullets: Abby Martin — first U.S. journalist to interview Cuban president Díaz-Canel — tells Mehdi U.S. sanctions are collapsing Cuba's grid: 'mass murder is the policy.'
Her documentary 'Cuba After Castro' connects Cuba to Gaza, which she calls the 'connective tissue' of U.S. foreign-policy harm.

items.subject: Meet the Man Who Could Cost Candace Owens Millions
bullets: Students for Trump founder Ryan Fournier was arraigned on six new counts — his first felony — for obstruction, assault and threats, atop cocaine and gun cases.
Main story: the self-proclaimed son of the Zodiac killer whose defamation case could cost Candace Owens millions.

items.newsletter: Bill Kristol & Andrew Egger - The Bulwark
items.subject: An American Life Cut Short
bullets: Kristol on Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, killed by ICE agents while driving to work in Houston — immigrants as keepers of the work-and-family American creed.
Platner suspended his campaign in a long, apology-free video; Maine Democrats plan a ~600-delegate convention to pick his replacement.

items.subject: 💣 Iran Humiliates Trump Again as His Imperial Bloodlust Surges
bullets: The U.S. and Iran are exchanging strikes again as a 'ceasefire that never really began' collapses; a senior U.S. official on Trump: 'Nobody's afraid of him right now.'
After two chaotic NATO summit days in Ankara, Trump flew home to a government and adversaries that increasingly tune out his threats.

items.subject: The Heat Is On
bullets: Extreme heat forced early closure of Trump's Great American State Fair — 44 people treated, 11 hospitalized — after 'virtually impossible without climate change' heat waves in the US and Europe.
Krugman: focusing on average warming misleads — a few degrees means a huge jump in extreme-event frequency, and the social and economic bill is arriving now.

items.subject: Birthright Citizenship Is Not Just for Immigrants
bullets: The Supreme Court’s recent decision in Trump v. Barbara reaffirmed birthright citizenship, citing the Fourteenth Amendment’s original intent and the 1898 Wong Kim Ark precedent, reinforcing equality for nearly all children born on U.S. soil.

The author argues that congressional legislation cannot overturn this constitutional ruling, warning that attempts by figures like Speaker Mike Johnson to reverse it ignore the finality of Supreme Court interpretations.
By upholding birthright citizenship, the Court preserves a core democratic principle, countering anti‑immigrant rhetoric and preventing a shift toward citizenship based on race, ancestry, or wealth.

items.subject: Meet Fox's Latest Favorite Primetime Guest: A Democratic Senator
bullets: Fox’s primetime lineup repeatedly attacked Pennsylvania Democrat John Fetterman during the 2022 midterms, calling him a “giant walking vegetable,” an “incompetent husk” after his stroke, and falsely labeling his policies as “pro‑murderer” and drug‑legalization.
Despite that earlier smear campaign, Fox now regularly invites Fetterman as a guest, turning him into a favored voice on the network’s MAGA‑aligned shows.

items.subject: He Lived Here for 35 Years. Put Three Kids in College. ICE Killed Him.
bullets: Araujo’s son, teacher Ronaldo Salgado, highlighted his father’s work ethic, three U.S.-born sons’ college education, and the family’s grief, while Rep. Sylvia Garcia and Rep. Christian Menefee condemned the killing and urged accountability

items.subject: The Pain in Spain is Mainly in Trump's Brain
bullets: Trump ordered Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent to cut off all trade with Spain, but this is legally and practically impossible due to EU membership and U.S. trade dependencies.
Krugman calls Trump's actions irrational, arguing no functioning political system would tolerate a leader making unilateral, nonsensical trade demands.
The crisis reflects a deeper failure in U.S. governance, as institutions enable a 'personality cult' around an unstable figure rather than challenging his authority.

items.subject: How the U.S.-Iran Fight in the Strait of Hormuz Can Be Resolved Before It Blows Up the MOU
bullets: The U.S. struck 80 Iranian targets and reimposed oil sanctions after Iran fired on ships in the Strait of Hormuz, while the IRGC claimed attacks on 85 U.S. sites, escalating the dispute over MOU interpretation.

Washington and Tehran disagree on whether the MOU requires vessels to coordinate with Iran; a proposed compromise would have ships notify both Iran and a GCC maritime authority, but talks stalled after Khamenei’s funeral.
The U.S. aims to assert escalation dominance, but rescinding the Iranian oil license may undermine its leverage if buyers view access as politically unstable.

items.subject: ‘We’re Gonna Be Killed By the Dumbest Fascists on Earth’: Mehdi & Wajahat Ali Laugh and Lament Over the Future of …
bullets: Mehdi and Wajahat Ali argue immigrants are vital to America, with Waj’s memoir “Go Back to Where You Came From” warning of backlash against immigrants in their homeland and sharing personal stories of Pakistani and Pakistani‑American heritage.

They critique current U.S. politics, contrasting Zohran Mamdani’s hopeful 4th‑of‑July speech with Donald Trump’s negative one, and recount MAGA crowds dismissing weather reports as “liberal” conspiracies during a storm‑hit event.
The conversation also covers Waj’s parents’ imprisonment, Trump’s interference in the World Cup, U.S. versus UK extremism, rising Islamophobia, and a brief mention of the Epstein Files.

items.newsletter: Here Be Dragons from Barbara F. Walter
items.subject: Call Your Friend
bullets: Studies of thousands of people show that strong relationships boost health, longevity, and life satisfaction, yet Americans spend less time with friends than a generation ago, driving a national loneliness epidemic.

Cross‑cultural observations—from the Huaorani chief in Ecuador to laborers in Laos—demonstrate that people in precarious environments invest deeply in relationships because life’s fragility makes each bond feel high stakes.

The author urges readers to seize the slower summer months to intentionally nurture friendships—calling a friend, inviting over, or simply chatting with a stranger—before life’s demands erode those connections.

items.newsletter: Bill Kristol & Andrew Egger - The Bulwark
items.subject: ICE Is Shooting People Again
bullets: U.S. Central Command announced new strikes on Iran after alleged attacks on commercial shipping, with Trump saying the cease‑fire is over while still urging negotiations for a new one.

ICE faced scrutiny after two deaths: Afghan veteran Mohammad Nazeer Paktiawal died in custody from an alleged drug reaction despite his wife’s pleas for his inhaler, and Mexican construction worker Lorenzo Salgado Araujo was killed during a disputed vehicle stop, with DHS providing no evidence of a “weaponized” vehicle.
The newsletter links these incidents to Trump’s aggressive mass‑deportation agenda, noting 21 ICE deaths recorded in 2026 and accusing the administration of institutionalized cruelty.

items.subject: ‘Not AIPAC’s Guy’
bullets: President Trump publicly doubted the interim Iran nuclear agreement, called the ceasefire “over,” and labeled talks with Tehran a waste, yet said he’d still permit negotiations after U.S. strikes in the Strait of Hormuz

The Michigan Democratic Senate primary has narrowed to Abdul El‑Sayed versus Rep. Haley Stevens, with Stevens portrayed as backed by AIPAC and Senate leader Chuck Schumer and criticized for accepting big‑tech and corporate donations

During the debate, the candidates clashed over U.S. policy toward Israel, with El‑Sayed accusing Stevens of being beholden to pro‑Israel interests while she defended her record and dismissed his critiques

items.newsletter: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
items.subject: Can a Democrat Really Win Texas?
bullets: Democrats see a tighter Texas race this year, with a NYT poll showing Rep. James Talarico and Gov. Greg Abbott dead‑locked at 47% each, despite the GOP planning roughly $500 million in negative ads against Talarico.
Pfeiffer argues Democrats have a better chance than in 2018 because of three factors he outlines, though the email cuts off before naming them.
He notes the emotional toll of past Texas losses, citing Beto O’Rourke’s 2018 campaign as a high point that raised hopes but ultimately fell short.

items.subject: Pathetic in Ankara
bullets: European leaders, frustrated by Trump’s renewed Greenland demand, are accelerating a “de‑Americanization” push, dropping US tech like Microsoft and investing hundreds of billions in homegrown AI, space and data infrastructure.

Trump’s failures in Iran, the near‑total cut of US aid to Ukraine (99%) and the ineffective tariff war have shown the limits of US military, financial and economic power, prompting allies to seek alternatives such as crypto, Chinese yuan and independent pipelines.
Consequently, NATO’s Ankara summit is expected to produce little, as allies now treat Trump as a senile blusterer and focus on building a world less dependent on US influence.

items.newsletter: Tim, Sarah and JVL - The Bulwark
items.subject: Even Marjorie Taylor Greene Doesn’t Buy the McConnell Story
bullets: Mitch McConnell is reportedly alive and taking calls, contradicting Laura Loomer’s claims, while the Bulwark notes the political fallout from the Platner candidacy and its impact on the movement and Maine’s future.
Kevin McCarthy and other Trump defenders are portrayed as losing moral footing after the Platner debacle, illustrating the broader crisis among Trump loyalists.
Mallory McMorrow’s Michigan Senate loss highlights the Democratic Party’s struggle to balance moderate and progressive factions, underscoring intra-party tensions.

items.subject: Why Mallory McMorrow’s Campaign Didn’t Survive
bullets: Mallory McMorrow, a 38‑year‑old state senator who launched a high‑profile Senate bid in April 2025 with viral LGBTQ‑rights speech and a video touting wins in red districts, fell to double‑digit poll deficits behind Abdul El‑Sayed and Haley Stevens by early July and suspended her campaign before the Aug. 4 primary.

Her collapse is attributed to establishment pressure from figures like retiring Sen. Gary Peters to cede her base to Stevens, internal polling suggesting El‑Sayed could capture her supporters, and her criticism of Democratic leadership that alienated party insiders.

items.subject: World’s Worst Person Is Also World’s First Trillionaire. What Does That Mean for the Rest of Us?
bullets: Elon Musk’s recent trillionaire status is attributed to a mix of government subsidies, unrealistic IPO promises, and political corruption, raising concerns about wealth-driven impunity and democratic erosion.
The interview critiques Musk’s SpaceX IPO goals—space tourism and AI data centers in space—as wildly unrealistic, and highlights his feud with Rep. Ro Khanna over deaths linked to DOGE’s USAID cuts.
Peter Thiel’s claim that democracy and freedom are incompatible is discussed, alongside protests in Albania over a Trump-linked resort plan on a protected nature reserve.

items.subject: MAGA Loves Trump’s World Cup Meddling—Until We Lose
bullets: Trump called FIFA president Gianni Infantino to lift a one‑game suspension on American‑born striker Folari Balogun, who had received a red card in a prior match, sparking a brief surge of pro‑Trump soccer enthusiasm that ended when the U.S. lost 4‑1 to Belgium and the movement quickly abandoned the sport

MAGA figures such as Benny Johnson, Dave Portnoy, and Laura Loomer celebrated Trump’s intervention, posted paid ads, and later mocked the U.S. defeat, while conservative commentators like Alex Marlow and Tyler Bowyer framed the incident as a victory for American citizenship and presidential power

items.subject: BREAKING: Nigel Farage 'Resigns' From UK Parliament Amid Investigations Into His Finances
bullets: Nigel Farage resigned as MP for Clacton to trigger a by‑election, citing a need for a fresh mandate while two parliamentary standards investigations probe his undeclared £5 million gift from a billionaire donor and alleged support from convicted fraudster George Cottrell.

The investigations will pause during the campaign but resume if Farage is re‑elected; the move will cost taxpayers a by‑election less than two years after his election, drawing criticism from political rivals who label him a “grifter” and accuse him of using the election to distract from financial misconduct.

items.newsletter: Bill Kristol & Andrew Egger - The Bulwark
items.subject: A Case Study in Recklessly Endangering the Senate
bullets: A former Maine Democratic Senate candidate, Graham Platner, was accused by a former girlfriend of a 2019 sexual assault, with Facebook messages and a second ex‑boyfriend corroborating her claim, prompting his likely withdrawal before the July 13 deadline.
Democrats fear Platner’s scandal could jeopardize Senate control and damage party credibility, as insiders warn replacing him mid‑race is difficult and may fuel conspiracy narratives among voters.
Bill Kristol and Ben Parker will discuss the fallout and broader implications on their Morning Shots Live show at 10:30 a.m. EDT today.

items.subject: Will Platner Drop Out?
bullets: Maine Democratic Party can replace him on the ballot if he exits by July 13, with a new candidate selected by July 27, otherwise the race remains unchanged

items.newsletter: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
items.subject: Thoughts on Graham Platner's Imploding Campaign
bullets: Jenny Racicot accused Democratic Senate nominee Graham Platner of 2021 sexual assault, with Politico detailing her account of non-consensual encounter.
Dan Pfeiffer admits supporting Platner was a mistake, urging him to drop out to allow a Democratic replacement in Maine against Susan Collins.
Allegations include Platner entering Racicot's home uninvited, intoxicated, and using force during the assault, per Politico's reporting.

items.subject: Day Off
bullets: Paul Krugman took a day off due to a crashing headache, attributing it to EU-KLEMS data analysis.
He anticipates returning to his usual schedule tomorrow.

items.subject: Blue Chips
bullets: Doomberg predicted AI data centers would need massive behind‑meter power, favoring natural‑gas turbines over nuclear due to speed and baseload reliability, and warned gas turbine supply would tighten.

Regulators have slowed off‑grid projects and Permian gas prices stayed negative in 2026, but Project Kilby—twice the size of Project Stargate—appears near final investment decision and could shift Permian oil‑gas dynamics.

items.subject: Who Should You Believe About the Genocide in Gaza? Mehdi Names Them All in 60 Seconds
bullets: Mehdi’s 60‑second rant lists the sources to trust on Gaza, contrasting the UN report that says Israel deliberately targeted children with Israel’s Foreign Ministry calling the report propaganda and a political blood libel.
He argues Israel and its allies routinely label accusations of war crimes, ethnic cleansing, and genocide as lies and antisemitism, urging readers to scrutinize who is speaking.

items.subject: Graham Platner Crossed the Voters’ Red Line
bullets: Graham Platner, the Democratic nominee for Maine’s Senate seat, faced a sexual assault allegation from his ex‑girlfriend claiming he forced her to have sex nearly five years ago, as reported by POLITICO and the New York Times.

Platner released a video denying the allegations but did not clarify whether he would withdraw from the race, despite focus groups showing Maine voters, especially women who supported Kamala Harris, would likely abandon him over such a scandal.

Prior to the allegations, Platner’s conduct—including a Nazi tattoo and reports of toxic behavior—had already sparked controversy, yet many voters in a June 3 focus group remained willing to support him until the accusations surfaced.

items.subject: BREAKING: Graham Platner Accused of Sexual Assault
bullets: Graham Platner, Maine’s Democratic Senate nominee, is accused by Jenny Racicot of forcibly assaulting her in 2021 after arriving drunk at her home, a claim he denies and has led to event cancellations and calls for his withdrawal before the July 13 deadline.

Platner’s campaign, already tainted by past scandals—including a Nazi‑style tattoo, misogynist Reddit posts, sexting after marriage, and a 2013‑15 intimidation allegation—has seen support from high‑profile progressives but limited Democratic fundraising, leaving Republican incumbent Susan Collins in a strong position.

items.subject: Ode to JVL
bullets: JVL’s mother‑in‑law died, prompting him to pause The Triad and devote time to his family, revealing his deep, often surprising devotion to loved ones; his wife and friends note his rare, intense care for her.
JVL’s close friendship with the newsletter’s author and co‑hosts—Tim and Bill—has evolved from a professional partnership into a tight personal bond, with constant text chats blending work and life.
The author urges readers to support the late mother‑in‑law’s favorite charity, highlighting JVL’s ongoing commitment to his community and the emotional impact of his generosity.

items.subject: LAST CHANCE TO SAVE 25% OFF! Zeteo Sale Ends Tomorrow
bullets: Zeteo’s annual subscription is discounted 25% to $5.25/month, ending tomorrow at midnight (July 7).
Paid subscribers gain full access to exclusive interviews, podcasts, documentaries, live Q&As, and events.
The offer emphasizes supporting independent journalism against billionaire‑owned media.

items.newsletter: Bill Kristol & Andrew Egger - The Bulwark
items.subject: America’s 250th Survived Trump’s Sabotage
bullets: Trump tried to hijack the 250th anniversary with a Mt. Rushmore video and a Fourth of July spectacle, but his efforts were eclipsed by historic symbols, the World Cup, and Pope Francis’s Liberty Medal speech praising America’s liberty legacy.

The author argues that the true spirit of the anniversary is embodied by figures like Pope Francis, Ukraine’s Zelensky and Hungary’s new PM Péter Magyar, who uphold liberal democracy over Trump’s self‑aggrandizement

Trump’s post‑holiday barrage of 100+ Truth Social posts and his upcoming NATO summit trip underscore his frantic attempts to stay relevant, while critics warn his actions could further damage U.S. standing in the alliance

items.subject: Kidnapped Gaza Doctor Faces ‘Life-Threatening Condition’ in Israeli Captivity
bullets: Dr. Hussam Abu Safiya, a prominent Gaza pediatrician abducted in Dec 2024, is in a life‑threatening condition after daily beatings, including a hammer attack, while held in Israeli administrative detention for 18 months without charge

His lawyer reports severe bruising, breathing difficulties and loss of consciousness, and the Israeli Supreme Court has been ordered to respond to a petition demanding his release and an independent prison inspection
The WHO, UN rapporteurs, Amnesty International and several UK MPs have called for his immediate release, while US leaders have remained silent

items.subject: First Draft: In Michigan, the War for the Democratic Party Is On
bullets: Mallory McMorrow dropped out of Michigan’s Democratic Senate primary without endorsing, prompting progressive leaders like Adam Green to hail Abdul El‑Sayed as the most important primary candidate in America","El‑Sayed, a pro‑Palestine progressive, leads polls over establishment‑backed Haley Stevens, who enjoys strong AIPAC support and ties to former Obama adviser David Axelrod","The race mirrors recent left‑wing primary victories in New York and Colorado, centering on Medicare for All and opposition to pro‑Israel lobby influence

items.newsletter: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
items.subject: Michigan and the Fight for the Future of the Party
bullets: The Michigan Senate primary, crucial for Democrats’ Senate majority, narrowed to a two‑way race after Mallory McMorrow dropped out, leaving El‑Sayed and Haley Stevens to compete.
Mike Rogers, who lost to Elissa Slotkin by under 20,000 votes, is now the unopposed Republican nominee for the open Michigan seat, making the Democratic contest decisive.
The primary has become a proxy battle over party divisions—establishment anger, ideological splits, and the Israel‑Gaza stance—highlighting the stakes for the party’s future.

items.subject: Pump and Dump and Trump
bullets: Donald Trump’s Trumpcoin lost 97% of its value, wiping out $3.8 billion for nearly one million retail investors, while insiders profited from early sales before the crash.

Trump’s administration’s pro‑crypto rhetoric pushed the market cap from ~$2 trillion to >$4 trillion after the 2024 election, only to see Bitcoin prices halve and the crypto market lose roughly $2 trillion in value, a modern pump‑and‑dump on unprecedented scale.

items.subject: Voters are Sounding More and More Like Trump on Birthright Citizenship
bullets: The Supreme Court upheld birthright citizenship in a 5‑4 ruling, rejecting Trump’s executive order as unconstitutional, despite justices appearing divided on the issue.
A focus group of Trump‑voting Florida women revealed strong skepticism of the 160‑year‑old precedent, framing immigration as a zero‑sum threat while still supporting “legal” pathways for newcomers.
The author argues that Trump’s failed legal push has shifted Republican rank‑and‑file rhetoric to mirror his hard‑line immigration stance, making it a litmus test for future GOP candidates.

items.subject: Our flash sale ends tonight at midnight.
bullets: The Bulwark’s July Fourth flash sale ends at midnight, offering a $86 annual membership for unlimited access to articles, newsletters, ad‑free shows, and community features.
The promotion frames the membership as a way to support independent journalism that tracks Trump‑era chaos and upcoming midterm elections.
The offer emphasizes patriotism as defending democracy and a free press, urging readers to ‘ride together’ against politicized symbols.

items.newsletter: Zeteo | Dark Side of the World Cup
items.subject: How Shameful Can the World Cup Get? We Take a Look at Two Previous Tournaments
bullets: The episode examines FIFA’s history of supporting authoritarian regimes, from Mussolini’s Italy in 1934 and Argentina’s 1978 military junta to recent scandals in Russia 2018 and Qatar 2022.
It highlights recent U.S. actions that undermined inclusivity, such as the unfair treatment of Iran’s team and the visa denial for a prominent Congolese fan, exposing ongoing hypocrisy.

items.subject: European vs. U.S. Economic Performance: An Update
bullets: European GDP per capita and productivity gaps with the U.S. have not widened over 25 years, contradicting claims of relative decline.
The 'US-EU paradox' arises from conflicting growth vs. purchasing power metrics, with industrial mix (U.S. tech concentration) and measurement challenges skewing comparisons.
Europe’s economic security, lower inequality, and social policies offset its tech-sector weaknesses, maintaining its global democratic and economic influence.

items.subject: Donald Trump Is Bringing Back ‘Death Panels’
bullets: In 2009 the ACA included a bipartisan provision allowing doctors to bill Medicare for end‑of‑life planning talks, which conservatives like Elizabeth McCaughey falsely portrayed as mandatory “death panels,” a claim that persisted with 40% of Americans believing it in 2010.
Trump’s administration is now reviving the “death panel” narrative by proposing new regulations that echo the earlier false accusations, positioning the policy as a threat to patient autonomy.

items.subject: Let Us Celebrate a 250-Year History of American Radicalism
bullets: The 250th anniversary of the U.S. founding coincides with Trump's reactionary policies, contrasting with the nation's radical egalitarian roots. 50 years of class-warfare by elites and neoliberal Democrats have eroded workers' rights, deepened inequality, and fueled authoritarianism. Progressives should seize the moment to reclaim the Democratic Party and revive the revolutionary promise of 'We the People.'

items.subject: This Week in Palestine: 1,000 Days of Genocide, as Israel Continues to Kill Kids
bullets: The newsletter marks 1,000 days of Israel’s war on Gaza, citing daily bombings, forced displacement, starvation, and medical deprivation that have killed dozens of children, including infant Siwar, and caused widespread atrocities such as torture, rape, and burning alive.

It argues the ongoing genocide is sustained by U.S. and Western support for Israel, noting recent incidents like the killing of three children and a footballer, and calls for international accountability.
The piece highlights recent developments, including the arrest of far‑right minister Itamar Ben‑Gvir and a plea from the Hind Rajab Foundation urging the U.S. to act amid the escalating violence.

items.subject: “As a writer, I often felt like I was trying to enter various clubs that didn’t want me”
bullets: Maggie Rogers describes a post‑COVID creative renaissance, returning to her studio after six years, experimenting with song structures and embracing vulnerability to produce an evolving album with multiple titles.

William T. Vollmann, battling cancer and opioid‑induced fog, confirms he will not finish his long‑running Seven Dreams series, citing time constraints and publisher conflicts, and is instead focusing on two short non‑fiction works.

items.subject: Heather Cox Richardson: America's 250 Great (and Awful) Years
bullets: Heather Cox Richardson argues that July 4th should be celebrated as a chance to reflect on America’s 250-year history, noting voters’ mixed feelings about patriotism amid post‑Trump rebuilding efforts.
She highlights the 250‑to‑250 project, which chronicles key people, places, and events that have shaped the U.S. toward a more perfect Union, offering lessons for contemporary politics.

items.subject: America at 250: The Founding and the Uniform
bullets: Jay Nordlinger argues America's global influence hinges on upholding its founding ideals of liberty and equality as it marks its 250th anniversary. Phil Edwards reports on The Villages, a Florida retirement community dubbed a 'baby boomer utopia.' Substack readers recommend Ken Burns documentaries like 'The American Revolution' and 'The Statue of Liberty' to reflect on U.S. history ahead of the 250th anniversary.

items.subject: Bicentennial Memories
bullets: Krugman recalls 1976 Lisbon bicentennial picnic, contrasting 1970s optimism with today’s grim 250th anniversary amid Trump-era corruption and political dysfunction.
1976’s hope stemmed from Vietnam’s end and Nixon’s Watergate downfall, which proved accountability; now, GOP enablers shield Trump despite unprecedented corruption.
Portugal’s 1974 revolution to democracy and U.S. low profile in Lisbon highlight 1976’s cautious optimism, absent in today’s polarized America.

items.subject: Celebrating Nationalism Is ‘Very Dangerous,’ Warns UCLA Historian
bullets: Historian Robin D. G. Kelley warns that celebrating nationalism during the U.S. 250th anniversary is dangerous, arguing the Declaration’s promise of equality was never meant for all, especially African‑American and Indigenous peoples.
He links Trump’s assault on birthright citizenship to a broader vision of a white republic and ties the rise of U.S. fascism to the systemic oppression of Palestinian voices.
Kelley questions whether the Founding Fathers left Britain for liberty or to preserve slavery, suggesting the nation must become “post‑nationalist” to confront these contradictions.

items.subject: On 'Piers Uncensored,' Mehdi Debates Director of Elon Musk-Backed, Anti-Muslim Movie
bullets: Mehdi Debates Uwe Boll on Piers Morgan’s ‘Uncensored’, accusing Boll’s film *Citizen Vigilante* of being a 90‑minute far‑right propaganda piece that is violent, incoherent and Islamophobic, and condemning Elon Musk for promoting it on Twitter.

Debate highlights Boll’s gaslighting of European crime rates, his self‑serving reversal on Donald Trump and MAGA, and the film’s refusal of classification in Germany, with Variety calling it a morally bankrupt exploitation movie.

items.subject: The American Popularity Contest Turns 250
bullets: The newsletter uses Robert Altman’s 1975 film *Nashville* to argue that America’s political scene is driven by celebrity culture, with characters chasing fame rather than policy, exemplified by the “Replacement” party’s gimmicky campaign.

It contrasts *Nashville* with Richard Linklater’s *Dazed and Confused*, suggesting both films show a persistent American desire for popularity and belonging, from 1976 bicentennial celebrations to high‑school cliques.
The author invites readers to share favorite American movies and promotes a Bulwark+ subscription discount for the Fourth of July weekend.

items.subject: It's 100x Worse Than Watergate—and Republicans Still Won't Quit
bullets: Sarah Longwell and Bill Kristol argue that the GOP has slid from Reagan’s inclusive rhetoric to a hard‑line stance, citing 2,000 daily ICE arrests and a backlash over the birthright citizenship ruling.
They claim the “socialist takeover” scare misreads Democratic voters, pointing to a shift toward moderate policies and highlighting emerging 2028 candidates who could reshape the party’s future.

items.subject: America at 250: We Had a Good Run
bullets: The author argues that America’s liberal arc has reversed, citing a surge toward illiberalism born of prosperity rather than crisis, and warns that the republic’s virtue may no longer be enough to save it.
He compares the nation’s decline to a hospice patient’s sudden illness, suggesting that the United States is now in a state of “deathbed vigil” as its liberal ideals erode.
He concludes that recognizing America’s vulnerability could inspire a renewed, bold struggle to reclaim liberalism, rather than accepting fatalistic decline.

items.subject: '250 Years of Cruelty' – Viet Thanh Nguyen on the ‘American Empire' and How to Save the U.S.
bullets: Viet Thanh Nguyen argues that the United States, on its 250th anniversary, is best described by cruelty, citing its long history of violence and oppression rather than optimism or hope.
He warns that the moral arc of the nation may bend toward greater injustice, pointing to systemic cruelty that has existed since the founding of America.
Nguyen calls for a critical reassessment of American identity and policies, urging readers to confront the nation’s violent legacy rather than romanticize its past.

items.subject: MAGA Goes Batshit Over SCOTUS Birthright Citizenship Ruling
bullets: The Supreme Court upheld birthright citizenship, sparking a wave of MAGA backlash that included Stephen Miller’s dehumanizing remarks about ‘shithole’ countries and calls for mass deportations and forced sterilization of foreign visitors.

Right‑wing influencers like Matt Walsh and Sean Davis urged harsher ICE enforcement, border militarization, and even the nullification of birthright citizenship, while claiming that female justices such as Amy Barrett were the worst on the Court.

Immigrant families—e.g., Estrella Lopez and Rosa—expressed fear that the ruling could lead to retroactive revocation of citizenship for children born to non‑citizen parents, highlighting the anxiety over potential future legal shifts and the impact on their families’ stability.

items.subject: 🇺🇸 Trump Throws America’s WORST Birthday Party
bullets: The USS Vincennes mistakenly shot down an Iranian passenger jet in 1988, killing 290 and later paying $62 million in damages to victims’ families.
Trump announced a July 4 speech on the National Mall despite forecast 97°F heat, delaying fireworks and banning backpacks, coolers, chairs, and bug spray for attendees.
Michigan’s Abdul El‑Sayed and Maine’s Graham Platner warn that AIPAC and campaign spending are fueling further Trump‑linked corruption.

items.subject: The End of North America
bullets: In what would be major news except for all the other disasters happening, Donald Trump has declined to renew the USMCA — the successor to the North American Free Trade Agreement — which he himself negotiated. This puts businesses on notice …

Some commentators have dismissed this as no big deal, because Trump’s successor will probably reverse his decision and make the USMCA permanent after all. However, this misses the point of such agreements. Before NAFTA went into effect, Nor…

items.subject: Treatment Resistant
bullets: Sanctions rarely achieve geopolitical goals and mainly harm civilian populations, acting as financial weapons of mass destruction, while targeted powers like China and Russia emerge stronger.
Huawei claims its new chip-design framework could reach 1.4‑nanometer density by 2031 without EUV tools, undermining U.S. concerns that sanctions can halt China's semiconductor progress.
EU attempts to replace Russian pipeline gas with pricier LNG are failing, as global energy demand ensures Russia’s oil and gas remain essential despite sanctions.

items.subject: Could This Outspoken Arizona Senator Be the Dems’ Presidential Nominee in 2028?
bullets: Senator Ruben Gallego, Arizona’s 2024 junior senator, says the Trump‑appointed DOJ is investigating him over alleged campaign‑finance violations because they’re “afraid” of his populist agenda to tax the rich and curb billionaire influence, including the Soros family.

He defends voting for the Laken Riley Act and a nuanced ICE stance—advocating dismantling the agency without endorsing the “Abolish ICE” slogan—while weighing whether to cut aid to Israel and hinting he may run for president in 2028.

items.subject: Air Conditioning is AMAZING. We Should Do It Better.
bullets: Happy Independence Day, everyone! In honor of America’s hot-AF 250th birthday, this week’s Receipts newsletter is an ode to air conditioning, a relatively modern invention that Americans take for granted and Europeans have snubbed. It’s eas…

While you’re sweating your guts out this weekend, I hope you’ll consider supporting one of civilization’s other great achievements: the free press. No better way to do that than by joining us as a Bulwark+ member. In fact, we’ve got a July …

items.subject: This Week in Democracy – Week 76: Trump Cashes In on Presidency as ICE Arrests 10,000 in 5 Days
bullets: Zeteo’s weekly round-up, documenting the growth of authoritarianism in Trump’s second term. Protesters march against Trump in front of the White House in Washington, DC, on June 27, 2026. Photo by Alex Wrobleski/AFP via Getty Images

This week, Donald Trump’s corruption was on full display, the Supreme Court granted him more king-like powers, and the U.S. president hosted an election-denying convict in the Oval Office. Happy 250th Birthday, America!
